[233:12]to do other things

[234:29]is contrary to peace.

[234:39]They rely on a constant low-level state of conflict and fighting to suppress their neighbors

[234:46]and also to preserve their freedom of action.

[234:58]then they can violate the airspace continuously.

[235:32]on peace and prosperity yet.

[235:42]As I said, two weeks ago, there is this ostensible agreement in place between Israel and Hamas.

[235:52]There do not appear to be any ground operations ongoing.

[235:55]They have withdrawn from certain key corridors and from certain cities.

[236:12]because there is still fighting.

[236:13]They are still restricting aid and resources.

[236:41]different locations, killing several IDF soldiers, and Israel retaliated with a series of airstrikes.

[236:48]Killing hundreds of people, causing hundreds of casualties, and it brought the total number

[236:53]of violations of the ceasefire to over 80.

[237:02]So, this is where it stands in Gaza. This is where it stands in the Middle East. The

[237:08]organization for Islamic cooperation, which is all the Muslim countries, and the Arab

[237:13]League, which is all the Arab countries, they all got together and endorsed this deal. The

[237:18]deal was brokered by Qatar, arguably imposed by the United States because they ran out

[237:23]of patience with Israel after Israel bombed the Hamas negotiators in Qatar weeks before

[237:37]personnel visiting Israel.

[237:46]was instrumental in brokering the deal and the original Abraham Accords because of his

[241:22]So we'll talk about this first, then we'll talk about Vance

[241:24]and what his political game was in Israel,

[241:27]because I think that's worth talking about.

[241:29]Here is strategically what is happening for the United States. You look around the world

[241:36]and the alarming development of which there are really two in particular, but one in general,

[241:43]what is happening in the world is the return of strategic competition, long-term strategic

[241:51]competition between great powers. This is something that was not the case prior to roughly

[241:59]From 1991 until 2015, our threat assessment as a country,

[242:05]we were preoccupied with proliferation of WMDs,

[242:10]rogue states, and terrorism.

[242:14]Those were the major threats.

[242:15]So we were worried about al-Qaeda.

[242:18]And Al Shabaab and groups like this, the Taliban, we were worried about rogue states like North

[242:23]Korea, like Serbia, like Iran.

[242:26]We were worried about the proliferation of weapons of mass destruction, chemical biological

[242:31]nuclear weapons.

[242:32]But after the fall of the Soviet Union, we didn't have to worry about a great power like

[242:37]we did in the previous centuries.

[242:38]We didn't have to worry about the Soviet Union, Nazi Germany, Great Britain, the French

[242:44]For the first time, and another way to say this is that our relative power was greater

[242:51]than ever.

[242:52]Our power relative to the next most powerful country was wider.

[242:59]That gap was wider than ever before in history.

[243:02]Nobody had achieved anything approaching equality, regional hegemony in any sphere of influence

[243:13]But the two particular developments which changed this is the rise of Russia, Russia rebuilt

[243:20]itself after the dismemberment and the fall and collapse of the Soviet Union.

[243:24]And China had its economic boom, which then began to translate into the projection of

[243:31]military power.

[243:32]And so this is the world we're living in now.

[243:34]And we have over committed our, so why are we going back that far?

[243:39]Because we have to understand that the landscape is changing and we are stretched out too thin.

[243:45]Our relative power is diminishing.

[243:49]America's power relative to the next, second and third most powerful countries, China and

[243:54]Russia is now non-existent.

[244:04]And so this is why the United States is becoming increasingly reliant on its allies, like NATO

[244:11]and Europe, like Egypt, Israel, Saudi Arabia, and the Emirates, and Turkey in the Middle

[244:18]East, India to counter China in the Indo-Pacific, Japan, Australia, and India in the Pacific

[244:30]This is why we're relying on our allies. We want to add on and supplement American power

[244:35]to balance Russia and China, which are on the rise. And what we're starting to realize

[244:42]is that we're stretched too thin. We're over committed. We cannot protect Europe from Russia.

[244:47]We cannot protect Israel from Iran and all of Iran's allies. We cannot protect Taiwan from

[244:56]If all of these wars happen simultaneously, we would not be able to defend all of our

[245:01]empire.

[245:03]One of them would have to give here.

[245:06]We could not deny Ukraine, or rather, we could not deny Russia trying to get Ukraine.

[245:12]We could not deny Iran attacking Israel.

[245:15]We could not deny China trying to invade Taiwan all at the same time.

[245:22]And we're starting to say that China is really going to be the biggest problem now, the biggest

[245:26]shipbuilding industry by far, the biggest economy they're competing with us with AI,

[245:32]that the biggest Navy in the world, their Navy in the Pacific is bigger than our Navy

[245:36]in the Pacific.

[245:38]They're building amphibious landing boats to land on the shores of Taiwan.

[245:42]And so we're starting to prioritize Asia, because increasingly, not only is that where

[245:47]the strategic threat is, but that's also where the strategic interests are.

[245:53]The chips are made in Taiwan, and that's vital for everything in the economy.

[245:57]The rare earth minerals and many other critical minerals are mostly mined and processed in

[246:02]China.

[246:03]And as China, India, and the other Asian Tigers

[246:06]take up a larger percentage of the economy,

[246:09]that's where all the critical trade is happening, too.

[246:13]And so this is where we're trying to get out of Europe.

[246:15]We're trying to get out of the Middle East.

[246:17]And we're trying to focus on China,

[246:19]because they're the biggest, most powerful country.

[246:22]And that, increasingly, is where the most vital commodities

[246:25]and trade is happening.

[246:27]How do we do that?

[246:30]Well, this is where Trump comes in.

[246:33]And Trump wants NATO to increase its defense budget.

[246:36]He wants Germany, France, and the UK to actually build a military, because if they can defend

[246:41]themselves from Russia, then we don't have to, and we don't have to worry about it.

[246:47]And we're trying to do the same thing in the Middle East.

[246:51]We still need the Middle East to be stable.

[246:53]We still want to maintain control of that region because you still have oil and gas there.

[247:03]But we're, we're passing it off to less reliable allies.

[247:08]In Europe, we are totally aligned with, let's say Germany, France, Italy, the UK, with the

[247:14]European Union, with the NATO countries there.

[247:16]We have an international architecture.

[247:18]They have the monetary union.

[247:21]They're basically solid.

[247:28]It's because Israel's a pariah state,

[247:30]and they're maybe our most reliable ally.

[247:35]That's according to American strategic thinking.

[247:40]They have the advanced defense industry.

[247:42]They have maybe the most competent military and intelligence.

[247:47]They're the closest in some ways to the United States.

[247:57]like Iraq, like Egypt and Jordan.

[249:54]Now, why do I explain all this?

[249:56]Because this is so critical to understanding why Israel is hostile to our foreign policy.

[250:04]Why they're not just like any other country or a bad ally, but they're actually contrary.

[250:11]They're actually hostile to and against our interests.

[250:15]Because keep in mind, think about it in these terms.

[250:19]How can we give security responsibilities to Israel if we can't trust them?

[250:27]Hello, to bring it back to the former point, if we have to send our top diplomatic personnel

[250:41]we would have no control over them, then how can we give them the whole Middle East?

[250:47]and be confident that they will be dutiful American clients.

[250:54]It doesn't work.

[250:57]There's no way.

[250:59]If anything, Israel will only get more powerful under that arrangement.

[251:04]They will have more power over their neighbors.

[251:06]They'll have more latitude and discretion to solve problems their way from the United States.

[251:16]When we are still heavily involved in the Middle East, when we're still deployed in Syria and Iraq, in Jordan, in Israel, actually,

[255:30]of how much money we have given to Israel.

[255:39]That doesn't factor in the war in Iraq.

[258:42]That's our alliance, our partnership as equals.

[260:09]That doesn't feel like a relation.

[266:45]the under 40 crowd believes in that anymore. They know they're going to need a new pitch,

[266:50]a new strategic pitch, a new ideological pitch for why we should maintain this relationship.

[266:57]And this is what they came up with. It was too obvious to have our leaders going there

[267:06]It was too obvious to give them all this money and say, they don't need us.

[267:12]That just didn't make sense.
